title: Pastéis de nata
subtitle: Portuguese custard tarts in puff pastry cups
excerpt: Portuguese custard tarts in the Lisbon style: puff pastry cups holding a thick egg-custard with a heavy hand of vanilla and cinnamon, baked at high heat until the tops blister black in spots. Twelve tarts.
sourceNotes: The pastéis de Belém form is documented from the 1830s onwards at the Jerónimos monastery in Lisbon; the recipe is closely guarded but the pre-1928 trade gives the basic technique (puff cups, set custard, high-heat finish). Portuguese household manuals from the late nineteenth century document the form. Maria de Lourdes Modesto's later catalogue of Portuguese cookery codified the home version that this recipe follows.

content.text: Pastéis de nata are a Lisbon classic. Small puff pastry cups hold a thick egg-yolk custard, baked at very high heat until the surface caramelises in dark spots. You form the cups by pressing rolled puff pastry into a muffin tin. You cook the custard on the hob to a thick pourable cream before it goes in. The bake is short and hot.

Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more. An oven thermometer takes the guessing out, especially for a bake that lives on the highest temperature the oven can hit. Pre-heat the empty tin in the oven so the bottom of each pastry cup starts cooking the moment it touches the metal.

items.name: Puff pastry
items.prepNote: all-butter, rolled to a 30×20 cm rectangle

content.text: Roll the {{puff-pastry}} into a 30×20 cm rectangle. Roll up tightly from the long edge into a sausage. Chill for 15 minutes. Cut the sausage into 12 equal slices (each about 2.5 cm). Set each slice cut-side up in a hole of a well-greased 12-hole muffin tin. Press flat to the base with your thumb and work the pastry up the sides so it forms a cup. Chill 15 minutes.

Heat the oven to 240°C fan, top shelf, with a baking tray on the shelf to pre-heat. Whisk the {{caster-sugar}} with the {{cornflour}} in a saucepan. Whisk in the {{whole-milk}} and {{double-cream}}. Cook over a medium heat, whisking constantly, until the mixture thickens to a thick pouring cream, about 5 minutes. Take off the heat. Whisk in the {{egg-yolks}} one at a time, then the {{vanilla-extract}}. Drop the long strips of {{lemon}} zest in to infuse, then fish them out after 5 minutes.

Fill each pastry cup with the warm custard, leaving 3 mm at the top. Set the muffin tin onto the pre-heated tray. Bake for 14 to 18 minutes; the pastry should be deep gold and the custard tops should blister in dark spots. Cool 5 minutes in the tin before lifting out with a small palette knife. Serve warm, dusted with {{cinnamon-ground}}.
